title: DoorDash — Food Delivery — System Design
description: Design a food-delivery platform coordinating three parties — eater, restaurant, courier. The themes are the order lifecycle state machine, dispatching couriers to orders (geospatial matching), live tracking and ETA, and keeping the three-sided flow consistent.

nodes.label: Eater Client
nodes.description: Browses restaurants, places orders, tracks delivery live.

nodes.label: Courier Client
nodes.description: Receives dispatch offers, sends location, updates delivery state.

nodes.label: Restaurant Client
nodes.description: Receives and confirms orders, marks food ready.

nodes.label: Order Service
nodes.description: Owns the order lifecycle state machine and orchestration.

nodes.label: Order Store
nodes.description: Authoritative orders + state transitions, sharded by order id.

nodes.label: Dispatch / Matching Service
nodes.description: Assigns couriers to ready orders optimizing time/cost.

nodes.label: Courier Geo Index
nodes.description: Live courier locations indexed by geo cell for nearby lookup.

nodes.label: Location Stream
nodes.description: High-frequency courier location updates, partitioned by region/cell.

nodes.label: Last-Known Location
nodes.description: Hot per-courier latest-position cache materialized from the stream; tracking reads this, not the log.

nodes.label: Order Events (Outbox)
nodes.description: Durable state-change events published from the transactional outbox; consumed by notifications, dispatch, and saga recovery.

nodes.label: Tracking Service
nodes.description: Serves live order/courier location + ETA to the eater.

nodes.label: ETA / Routing Service
nodes.description: Estimates prep + travel time for matching and display.

nodes.label: Notification Service
nodes.description: Pushes state changes to all three parties.

nodes.label: Payment Service
nodes.description: Authorizes at order, captures on completion.

functional: Place an order from a restaurant; pay for it.
Send the order to the restaurant; track prep status.
Dispatch a courier to pick up and deliver the order.
Live-track the courier and show an ETA to the eater.
Drive all three parties through the order lifecycle with notifications.
nonFunctional: Order correctness: the lifecycle never gets into an inconsistent state across parties.
Real-time matching and tracking (location updates within seconds).
Reliable: a crash mid-order must not lose or duplicate orders/payments.
Scale to millions of orders/day and many concurrent live couriers.
Dispatch quality: minimize delivery time/cost while staying timely.
Location privacy: courier GPS is sensitive — eaters see it only for an assigned, active order; retention is short and precision is reduced after delivery.
Graceful degradation: under map/ETA-provider failure, still accept orders, dispatch with degraded scoring, and show coarse ETAs rather than failing.

capacity.label: Orders per day
capacity.note: 2M orders/day averages ~23/s; a 5x dinner spike concentrated in ~4 peak hours pushes order-create QPS to ~115/s.

capacity.label: Live couriers
capacity.note: 100K couriers online at peak, each pinging GPS every 4s.

capacity.label: Location write rate
capacity.note: 100K couriers / 4s = 25K location updates/s steady at peak. This is the dominant write load — it goes to the stream and geo index, never per-ping to OrderDB (which would be ~25K writes/s of order-table churn).

capacity.label: Dispatch matches
capacity.note: One match per order at ~ready time; tied to order rate. Each match scores ~10–50 candidate couriers from the hot geo cell, so ~1–6K candidate scorings/s.

capacity.label: Tracking fanout
capacity.note: Active deliveries (orders in assigned→delivered, ~30–45 min each) at peak imply a few hundred K concurrent tracking connections, each pushed a throttled position every 2–4s.

capacity.label: Match latency
capacity.note: Assign a courier within a few seconds of food being ~ready.

capacity.label: Tracking freshness
capacity.note: Eater sees courier move near-real-time; pushes throttled to ~every 2–4s.

capacity.label: Storage
capacity.note: ~2M orders/day × small rows is modest and durable; the 25K/s location firehose is held in an ephemeral geo index + last-known cache, rebuildable from the stream.

api.description: Place an order; authorizes payment and starts the lifecycle. Idempotent via the Idempotency-Key header so a client retry never creates a second order or double-authorizes.
Get live order status, courier location, and ETA.
Courier app reports its GPS position frequently (~every 4s). Fire-and-forget into the location stream; never written per-ping to OrderDB.
Restaurant-driven lifecycle transition (confirm / preparing / ready / reject). A compare-and-set on the expected current state makes the transition safe under retries and concurrent actors.

Courier accepts a time-boxed offer. CAS from offered→accepted on the offer (rejected if expired or already taken) atomically flips order→assigned and courier→on_delivery, so two couriers can't both win.
Courier declines (or the offer times out). Frees the offer and dispatch re-offers the next candidate, incrementing the assignment-attempt count.
Courier-driven lifecycle transition (picked_up / in_transit / delivered) with compare-and-set on the expected state.
Eater cancels. Allowed branches differ by state: before prep releases the auth; after capture triggers a refund compensation. Idempotent.

dataModel.name: orders
dataModel.note: Authoritative order + lifecycle state, sharded by order id.

fields.name: state
fields.type: enum(placed, confirmed, preparing, ready, assigned, picked_up, in_transit, delivered, cancelled)

dataModel.name: order_state_transitions
dataModel.note: Append-only transition log: the saga ledger. Each row is one accepted state change; an order is replayed/resumed from here.

dataModel.name: idempotency_keys
dataModel.note: Records the result of each first-seen mutating request so a retry returns the same response instead of repeating the effect.

fields.name: request_hash
fields.type: varchar (guards key reuse with a different body)

dataModel.name: payment_attempts
dataModel.note: Auth/capture lifecycle per order — survives provider retries, ambiguous callbacks, and refunds.

dataModel.name: courier_offers
dataModel.note: Each dispatch offer is a lease with an expiry and a version; accept is a CAS from offered→accepted.

dataModel.name: outbox_events
dataModel.note: Transactional outbox: state-change events written in the same DB txn as the transition, then published to the stream. Guarantees 'transition persisted' and 'event emitted' don't split-brain.

dataModel.name: courier_location
dataModel.note: Live position in the geo index. Ephemeral/rebuildable; matching filters on status=available and a fresh updated_at (stale rows are dropped, not matched). No long GPS history is retained here — bounded retention only, for fraud/support.

patterns.name: Order state machine
patterns.what: Model the order as explicit states with allowed transitions, persisted durably.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-step, multi-party workflows that must stay consistent.

patterns.name: Geospatial courier index
patterns.what: Index live courier locations by geo cell for fast nearby lookup.
patterns.whenToUse: Matching that needs 'who's near here right now'.

patterns.name: Dispatch / matching
patterns.what: Score nearby available couriers (distance, ETA, load) and offer the order.
patterns.whenToUse: Assigning scarce mobile resources to time-sensitive tasks.

patterns.name: Location streaming + last-known
patterns.what: Ingest frequent GPS updates into a stream + a hot last-known store, not the DB per ping.
patterns.whenToUse: High-frequency location writes feeding tracking and matching.

patterns.name: Saga / compensation
patterns.what: Coordinate the multi-service flow with compensating actions on failure (refund, reassign).
patterns.whenToUse: Distributed workflows that can't be one ACID transaction.

patterns.name: Event-driven notifications
patterns.what: Emit state-change events that notify all three parties.

steps.title: 1. Naive: One Order Record, Manual Dispatch, No Tracking (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. The eater places an order; it's written as a single row (placed -> done); the restaurant sees it and cooks; a courier is picked by hand (or first-available) and the eater just waits with no live view.

It's correct for one order at a time, and it surfaces the real problems immediately. A double-tap or client retry creates two orders and authorizes payment twice — there's no idempotency. There's no notion of who's nearby, so dispatch can't be good or fast. There's no live courier location, so the eater can't track anything or get an ETA. And the flow silently spans payment, the restaurant, and the courier with no way to recover if one step fails partway.

So the baseline motivates the rest: model the order as an explicit lifecycle, index live courier locations, match couriers properly, stream tracking, and make the multi-party flow consistent under failure.

view.caption: The eater places an order through the API gateway to the order service, which persists state in the order store and sends the order to the restaurant client.

steps.decisionPrompt: If you assign couriers by hand and store an order as one row with no states, what breaks the moment you have thousands of concurrent orders and live couriers?
whyNow: Establishing the naive baseline first makes the rest obvious: every later step removes a specific pain here — no nearby lookup, no tracking, no failure recovery.

recap.after: An order can be placed and a restaurant notified — single-order, manual, no tracking.
recap.newRisk: No real-time courier locations, no good dispatch, no ETA/tracking, and no consistency across payment/restaurant/courier.

traps.trap: Treating the order as one mutable row with manual courier assignment.
traps.why: It can't express the multi-party lifecycle, can't match couriers by proximity, and has no recovery when a step fails midway.
traps.instead: Model an explicit order state machine, index live courier locations, and orchestrate the flow as a saga.

steps.title: 2. The Order Lifecycle State Machine
description: An order moves through explicit states: placed -> confirmed -> preparing -> ready -> assigned -> picked_up -> in_transit -> delivered (with cancel/refund branches). Model this as a state machine with allowed transitions, persisted durably as the source of truth.

Each transition is triggered by a specific party from a specific prior state. Restaurant: placed->confirmed->preparing->ready (or placed/confirmed->rejected). Courier: assigned->picked_up->in_transit->delivered. Eater: cancel is allowed pre-prep (release hold) but branches differently after capture (refund). Each transition emits an event that notifies the others; invalid transitions (wrong actor or wrong prior state) are rejected, and the order store is authoritative for 'what state is this order in'.
This is the backbone — dispatch, tracking, and payment all hang off state transitions, and consistency means the state machine is the one place the truth lives.

view.caption: The order service drives each state change in the order store and emits to the notification service, which pushes lifecycle updates back to the eater client.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you keep three parties (eater, restaurant, courier) agreeing on the order's status?

concepts.definition: Explicit states + allowed transitions persisted as the source of truth.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents inconsistent multi-party states and makes the flow auditable and recoverable.
concepts.example: ready -> assigned only after a courier accepts; transitions emit events.

whyNow: The state machine is the consistency backbone of a multi-party workflow; everything else triggers or reacts to its transitions.

recap.after: A durable, event-emitting order lifecycle all parties share.
recap.newRisk: Assigning a courier requires knowing who's nearby — a real-time geo problem.

steps.title: 3. Live Courier Locations (Geo Index)
description: Matching needs 'which available couriers are near this restaurant right now'. Couriers stream GPS frequently; index their live positions by geo cell (geohash/S2) so a nearby query is a cheap cell + neighbor lookup, not a scan.

Don't write every GPS ping to the order DB — that's a write storm (~25K writes/s at peak). Update a hot geo index (and the last-known cache) on each ping; the index is derived/ephemeral, rebuilt from the stream. Track recency: drop couriers whose `updated_at` is stale so matching never offers to a courier who went dark, and distinguish available vs on_delivery so busy couriers aren't re-offered. Don't retain full GPS history here — keep only the live position, with bounded retention elsewhere for fraud/support.
This reuses the geospatial-indexing pattern (geohash cells, neighbor expansion) from proximity search, applied to moving couriers.

view.caption: Couriers' GPS updates flow through the location stream into the courier geo index, which the dispatch/matching service queries for nearby couriers.

options.name: Geohash/S2 cell index in a KV store (default)
options.description: Each courier's position is encoded to a geohash/S2 cell and upserted in a KV store keyed by courier id, so a nearby query reads a cell plus its neighbors.
pros: Encode each courier's lat/lng to a geohash/S2 cell key, so 'nearby' is a cheap prefix/cell lookup.
Writes are simple upserts keyed by courier id, absorbing 100K pings every 4s.
Cells shard naturally by region, so dense cities scale horizontally.
cons: Fixed cell size means a sparse area returns too few and a dense area too many couriers - must query neighbor cells.
Boundary effects: the nearest courier can sit just across a cell edge.
Recency must be tracked separately so stale positions aren't matched.

view.caption: Each courier's position is encoded to a geohash/S2 cell and upserted in the KV-backed courier geo index that dispatch reads for nearby couriers.

options.name: In-memory geo store (Redis GEO)
options.description: Live courier positions are held in Redis GEO sets, so a nearby query is a GEORADIUS lookup served from memory in sub-millisecond time.
pros: GEOADD/GEORADIUS gives radius queries out of the box with sub-millisecond reads.
Holding only live courier positions in memory keeps the working set tiny and fast.
Simple to operate for the 'where are nearby couriers right now' query.
cons: Single-instance memory caps how many live couriers you hold; sharding GEO sets is manual.
Volatile - a restart loses positions until couriers re-ping.
High write rate (100K/4s) can saturate one node without careful sharding.

view.caption: Courier positions land in an in-memory Redis GEO store, so dispatch answers a nearby query with a single GEORADIUS lookup fed by the location stream.

options.name: Quadtree / adaptive spatial index
options.description: A quadtree subdivides space where couriers are dense and stays coarse where sparse, so each region query returns a balanced number of candidate couriers.
pros: Cells subdivide where couriers are dense, so each query returns a balanced candidate count.
Adapts to a city's uneven courier density better than fixed cells.
Good for k-nearest queries where you want exactly N candidates.
cons: Tree rebalancing under a flood of position updates is expensive and complex.
Harder to shard and replicate than a flat geohash keyspace.
Update-heavy workload (every courier every 4s) stresses the mutable tree.

view.caption: A quadtree index with adaptive cells holds courier positions from the location stream, returning a balanced candidate set to dispatch per region query.

steps.decisionPrompt: 100K couriers each send GPS every 4 seconds. Where do those updates go, and how do you query 'nearby'?

concepts.definition: Live positions bucketed by geo cell for fast nearby lookup.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns 'find nearby couriers' into a cell range query at high update volume.
concepts.example: Courier ping -> update its geohash cell; dispatch reads the restaurant's cell + neighbors.

whyNow: Knowing who's nearby in real time is the precondition for dispatch; the geo index is the mechanism and a write-storm-avoidance decision.

recap.after: Live courier positions queryable by area, cheaply.
recap.newRisk: Knowing who's nearby isn't matching — you must pick the right courier and offer the order.

steps.title: 4. Dispatch: Matching Couriers to Orders
description: When an order is (nearly) ready, dispatch finds candidate couriers from the geo index and scores them: distance/ETA to the restaurant, current load, direction, fairness. It offers the order to the best candidate.

Offers are time-boxed leases: each `courier_offers` row has an `expires_at` and a monotonic `offer_version`. Accept is a compare-and-set from offered→accepted that atomically flips the order to 'assigned' and the courier to on_delivery, so two near-simultaneous orders can't both claim one courier. A timeout worker expires stale offers and requeues the order to the next candidate, bumping `assignment_attempts` and preserving offer history. Matching filters the geo index on status=available and a fresh updated_at so stale or busy couriers aren't offered.
Timing matters: dispatch around when the food will be ready (using ETA), so the courier arrives just in time rather than waiting or being late — batching nearby orders is an optimization.

view.caption: The dispatch/matching service takes an order ready to assign, queries the courier geo index for nearby couriers, scores them via the ETA/routing service, and offers the delivery to a courier.

options.name: Greedy nearest-courier assignment (default)
options.description: When an order is ready, dispatch picks the single best-scoring nearby courier by distance and ETA and offers it immediately, claiming the courier atomically.
pros: When an order is ready, pick the best-scoring nearby courier (distance + ETA) and offer immediately - low latency.
Simple to reason about and easy to fall back to under load.
Claiming the courier atomically (compare-and-set on courier state) prevents double-booking.
cons: Locally optimal, globally poor: grabbing the nearest courier for one order can strand a better-fit order.
Two near-simultaneous orders can contend for the same courier, needing a lock/retry.
Ignores future orders, so it leaves the fleet poorly positioned.

view.caption: Dispatch greedily picks the single best-scoring nearby courier from the geo index, scores it via the ETA service, and offers that one courier the delivery.

options.name: Batched assignment (windowed matching)
options.description: Dispatch collects orders and couriers over a short window and solves them together as a batch (min-cost bipartite matching) for a globally better assignment.
pros: Collect orders and couriers over a short window, then solve as a batch (e.g. min-cost bipartite matching) for a globally better assignment.
Naturally avoids double-booking - one solver assigns each courier once.
Enables batching multiple pickups along one courier's route.
cons: Adds a few seconds of matching latency that's bad for a 'ready now' order.
The optimizer is far more complex to build, tune, and operate.
A late-arriving urgent order waits for the next window.

view.caption: A batch matcher collects orders and couriers over a window, scores candidates from the geo index against the ETA service, and offers couriers a globally optimized assignment.

options.name: Broadcast offer, couriers accept (pull)
options.description: Dispatch fans the order out as an offer to several nearby couriers at once, and the first to accept claims it atomically — no central optimizer decides.
pros: Offer the order to several nearby couriers and let the first to accept take it - no central optimizer.
Respects courier choice and works when courier availability is uncertain.
Simple to scale: dispatch just fans out offers.
cons: Races on acceptance need an atomic claim so two couriers don't both take it.
Unattractive orders go unaccepted, requiring re-offer and escalation logic.
Less control over global efficiency and ETA promises.

view.caption: An offer broadcaster reads nearby couriers from the geo index and fans the offer to several at once, letting the first courier to accept claim it.

steps.decisionPrompt: Two orders become ready near the same courier at once. How do you assign without double-booking or starving one?

concepts.definition: Score nearby couriers and offer with a time-boxed accept, re-offering on decline/expiry.
concepts.whyItMatters: Respects courier agency and avoids assigning to an unavailable/unwilling courier.
concepts.example: Offer to nearest available; 15s to accept; else next candidate.

whyNow: Dispatch is the logistics heart; the offer/accept protocol and ready-time-aware matching are the senior signals distinct from pure ride-matching.

recap.after: Couriers matched to orders via scored, time-boxed offers.
recap.newRisk: The eater wants to watch the courier approach — live tracking + ETA.

traps.trap: Assigning a courier the instant the order is placed.
traps.why: The courier idles at the restaurant while food cooks, wasting capacity.
traps.instead: Dispatch timed to the food's ready ETA.

flows.name: Match a courier (offer / accept)
flows.note: Find nearby couriers, score by ETA/load, and offer with a time-boxed accept.

steps.title: 5. Live Tracking and ETA
description: Once assigned, the eater watches the courier move. The tracking service serves the courier's latest position (from the location stream's last-known store) plus a live ETA from the routing/ETA service.

Push updates to the eater (over a live connection) as the courier moves, throttled — they don't need every GPS point, just smooth movement and a stable ETA. The same location stream feeds both dispatch (matching) and tracking (display).
ETA blends remaining prep time, courier travel time, and traffic — recomputed as the courier progresses.

view.caption: Courier pings flow into the location stream, which materializes a last-known-location cache; the tracking service reads that hot cache plus ETAs from the ETA/routing service, while the notification service pushes order state changes.

options.name: Server-push over a persistent connection (default)
options.description: The eater app holds a persistent WebSocket/SSE to the tracking service, which reads courier positions from the location stream and pushes them on movement.
pros: The eater app holds a WebSocket/SSE to the Tracking service and receives courier positions as they stream in - no polling.
Tracking reads live positions from the location stream, never the orders database, so map updates don't touch OrderDB.
Pushes only on movement, cutting redundant traffic versus fixed-interval polls.
cons: Maintaining millions of long-lived connections needs a connection/gateway tier.
Reconnect and backfill logic is required for flaky mobile networks.
More infrastructure than stateless polling.

view.caption: The eater app holds a persistent connection to the tracking service, which reads the latest position from the last-known cache (materialized from the stream), computes ETAs, and pushes updates through the notification service.

options.name: Client polls a cached position endpoint
options.description: The eater app polls a stateless endpoint every few seconds, and tracking serves the courier's last-known position from an in-memory cache rather than OrderDB.
pros: Stateless HTTP: the app polls every few seconds and Tracking serves the courier's last position from an in-memory cache.
No long-lived connections to manage - trivial to scale behind a load balancer.
Polling the cache (not OrderDB) still keeps the database off the hot path.
cons: Fixed-interval polling wastes requests when the courier is idle and lags when they move fast.
At millions of eaters, poll volume itself becomes a scaling concern.
Map feels less smooth than continuous push.

view.caption: The eater app polls the stateless tracking endpoint, which serves the courier's last-known position from the in-memory last-known cache plus a live ETA from the ETA/routing service.

steps.decisionPrompt: Should the eater's app poll the courier's GPS directly, and should each ping hit the database?
whyNow: Live tracking is the headline eater experience; serving it from the stream/last-known store (not the DB) is the scaling insight.

concepts.definition: Serve the freshest cached position and push throttled updates, not raw GPS or DB reads.
concepts.whyItMatters: Smooth real-time tracking without DB write/read storms.
concepts.example: Courier ping -> last-known store -> throttled push to the eater's open connection.

recap.after: Live, ETA-annotated courier tracking for the eater.
recap.newRisk: The multi-service flow can fail mid-way (payment, restaurant cancel, courier drops) and must stay consistent.

flows.name: Live tracking + ETA
flows.note: Pings land in the stream and materialize a last-known cache; the eater reads the latest position from that cache with a recomputed ETA.

steps.title: 6. Consistency Across Failures (Saga)
description: The flow spans order, payment, restaurant, and courier services — not one ACID transaction. Coordinate it as a saga: each step has a compensating action if a later step fails. Payment is authorized at order and captured on delivery; a cancel refunds the authorization.

Handle the real failure branches explicitly: restaurant rejects -> refund + notify; courier drops the order -> reassign; eater cancels before prep -> release the hold, after capture -> refund. Each transition is idempotent — keyed by an idempotency key stored in an `idempotency_keys` table and compare-and-set on the order's expected state/version — so retries and ambiguous payment-provider callbacks don't double-charge or double-assign.

The order state machine (in `order_state_transitions`) is the saga's ledger: it records where the flow is, so a crashed orchestrator resumes from the persisted state. State changes are emitted via a transactional outbox — the event is written in the same DB transaction as the transition and published from there — so a notification or dispatch trigger is never lost or duplicated relative to the state change.

view.caption: The order service authorizes/captures through the payment service and persists each transition to the order store in the same txn as an outbox event; the order-events stream then drives dispatch and notifications, so 'transition persisted' and 'event emitted' never split-brain.

options.name: Orchestrated saga with compensations (default)
options.description: A central order orchestrator drives each step — authorize payment, confirm restaurant, dispatch — and runs compensating actions like voiding the auth when a later step fails.
pros: A central order orchestrator drives steps (authorize payment, confirm restaurant, dispatch) and runs compensations on failure.
If the restaurant rejects after payment auth, the saga voids/refunds the authorization so the eater isn't charged.
One place to see and reason about the order's distributed transaction state.
cons: The orchestrator is a complex, critical component that must itself be highly available.
Compensations (void auth, cancel dispatch) must be carefully designed and idempotent.
Centralizing the flow can couple the services to the orchestrator.

view.caption: A central order orchestrator sequences the payment service, order store, dispatch, and notification service, running compensations when a later step fails.

options.name: Choreographed saga (events between services)
options.description: Each service reacts to events emitted by the others and emits its own, so the order flow and its compensations emerge from event handlers with no central coordinator.
pros: Each service reacts to events and emits its own - no central orchestrator to build or scale.
Loosely coupled: payment, restaurant, and dispatch evolve independently.
Naturally resilient - a slow consumer just processes its events later.
cons: The end-to-end flow is implicit across many handlers, making it hard to follow and debug.
Compensation chains (refund after a late rejection) are easy to get wrong with no central view.
Cyclic event dependencies can emerge as the flow grows.

view.caption: Each of the payment service, dispatch, and notification service reacts to and emits events around the order service and store, with no central coordinator driving the flow.

options.name: Auth-then-capture (delay the charge)
options.description: Payment is only authorized (held) at order time and captured after the restaurant confirms, so a pre-confirm rejection just releases the hold instead of refunding.
pros: Only authorize (hold) at order time and capture the charge after the restaurant confirms - a rejection just releases the hold, no refund.
Avoids the refund path entirely for the common reject-before-confirm case.
Maps cleanly to how card networks already work (auth vs capture).
cons: Holds expire, so a long delay before confirm can drop the authorization.
Still needs saga logic for failures after capture (e.g. courier never arrives).
Two-phase payment adds states to the order lifecycle to track.

view.caption: The order service authorizes a hold on the payment service and captures only after restaurant confirmation, persisting state and notifying as the order advances.

steps.decisionPrompt: Payment is authorized, the restaurant then rejects the order. What has to happen, and how do you avoid charging the eater?

concepts.definition: A multi-step distributed workflow where each step has a compensating undo on failure.
concepts.whyItMatters: Achieves cross-service consistency without a distributed ACID transaction.
concepts.example: Authorize -> restaurant rejects -> void authorization + notify eater.

whyNow: Multi-party consistency under partial failure is the correctness crux; the saga + idempotent transitions over the state machine is the standard answer.

recap.after: Failure branches compensated; idempotent, resumable transitions.
recap.newRisk: Meal-time spikes and location-write volume stress dispatch and the geo index.

failureDrills.scenario: The order orchestrator crashes after assigning a courier.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: On restart it reads the persisted order state and resumes; idempotency prevents a duplicate assignment.
failureDrills.mitigation: State machine as durable saga ledger + idempotent transitions.

failureDrills.scenario: A courier accepts then goes offline before pickup.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Dispatch detects the drop and re-offers the order to another courier, incrementing assignment_attempts and preserving offer history.
failureDrills.mitigation: Offer lease (expires_at) + timeout worker that requeues the order + reassignment compensation.

failureDrills.scenario: The payment provider times out; we don't know if the auth succeeded.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Retry with the same provider_payment_id (idempotency token); the PSP returns the original result rather than a second authorization.
failureDrills.mitigation: payment_attempts row with a stable provider idempotency token; reconcile via callback/inquiry, never blind-retry.

failureDrills.scenario: A client (or queue) retries the same lifecycle transition.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The first apply persists and emits; the retry is deduped by idempotency key and returns the stored result with no second effect.
failureDrills.mitigation: idempotency_keys table + CAS on expected state; outbox event written exactly once per transition.

flows.name: Saga compensation on failure
flows.note: A multi-party order coordinated with compensating actions when a step fails.

steps.title: 7. Scaling Spikes, Geo Hotspots, and Regions
description: Demand spikes at meal times and concentrates geographically (a dense city at dinner). Shard orders by id and the geo index by region/cell so a hot city's load spreads; dispatch runs per-region so matching is local.

Absorb the location-write firehose with the stream + last-known store (not per-ping DB writes), and scale tracking/dispatch consumers off it. Batch nearby orders to one courier when it improves efficiency under load.
The geo index and last-known store are rebuildable from the location stream; orders are the durable truth. Degrade gracefully under extreme load (longer ETAs, surge) rather than failing matches.

view.caption: Under load the location stream feeds the courier geo index that dispatch queries, the order service writes the order store, and tracking reads live positions from the stream.

options.name: Partition by region/cell with per-region dispatch (default)
options.description: Shard orders by id and the geo index + location stream by region/cell, and run a dispatch worker per region so matching is local and a hot city scales independently.
pros: A dense city's load spreads across cells and regional workers instead of one global matcher.
Stream partitions key by region/cell, so consumers scale per region with locality.
Most matches are intra-region, so candidate sets stay small and fast.
cons: Cross-region boundary orders (courier near an edge) need a hand-off or shared-edge query.
Rebalancing partitions as demand shifts (lunch downtown vs dinner suburbs) is operational work.
Per-region state must stay rebuildable from the stream after a worker restart.

view.caption: Region-partitioned streams feed per-region geo indexes and dispatch workers, with orders sharded by id in the store and tracking reading regional positions.

options.name: Dynamic hot-cell splitting
options.description: When a single geo cell gets too dense (stadium, downtown at dinner), split it into finer sub-cells so each query returns a balanced candidate count and no single cell becomes a write/read hotspot.
pros: Keeps candidate sets balanced even where couriers concentrate.
Avoids one giant cell becoming a single-key hotspot in the index.
Reverts to coarse cells off-peak, so off-hours stay cheap.
cons: Splitting/merging cells live adds index-management complexity.
Mid-split queries must span old and new cell boundaries carefully.
Thresholds need tuning per market to avoid thrashing.

view.caption: A dense cell is subdivided into finer sub-cells in the geo index so dispatch reads a balanced candidate set per query under load.

options.name: GPS-ping backpressure + degraded ETA mode
options.description: Under extreme load, shed and coalesce the location firehose (lower ping rate, drop redundant pings) and fall back to coarse/heuristic ETAs when the routing provider is rate-limited or down — accept and dispatch orders regardless.
pros: Backpressure on pings protects the stream and index from a thundering peak.
Coarse ETAs (distance/heuristic) keep dispatch and display working when the routing provider fails or throttles.
Orders still get accepted and matched rather than erroring out.
cons: Lower ping rate makes tracking slightly less smooth and matching slightly less precise.
Heuristic ETAs are less accurate, hurting promise quality temporarily.
Needs clear thresholds and recovery so it doesn't stay degraded.

view.caption: Under load the stream sheds/coalesces pings and dispatch scores against a degraded coarse-ETA service so orders keep flowing when the routing provider is throttled.

steps.decisionPrompt: Friday 7pm in a dense city: order volume and courier pings both spike. What breaks first and how do you scale it?
whyNow: Closing on meal-time spikes + geo hotspots + regional sharding shows the design scales where it actually gets stressed.

recap.after: Regionally-sharded, spike-tolerant dispatch + tracking; rebuildable geo state; degraded-mode fallback.
recap.newRisk: Surge/ETA degradation tradeoffs and cross-region edge cases — bounded by per-region dispatch and graceful degradation.

bottlenecks.mitigation: Partitioned stream + last-known cache; never per-ping DB writes; backpressure under extreme load.

bottlenecks.issue: Dispatch hotspot in a dense area at peak.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Per-region dispatch, dynamic hot-cell splitting, order batching, surge to balance supply/demand.

bottlenecks.issue: No visibility into match quality or staleness during a spike.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Track dispatch latency, offer-acceptance rate, stale-location %, ETA error, transition-failure rate, and saga-compensation rate as user-visible SLOs.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Food Delivery Platform
finalDesign.description: An order moves through a durable state machine (placed->...->delivered); each transition is persisted with an idempotency key and emits an event via a transactional outbox (the order-events stream) that drives notifications and dispatch — so 'persisted' and 'emitted' never diverge. Payment is authorized at order and captured on delivery, with saga compensation on failure branches. Couriers stream GPS into a region-partitioned location stream feeding a geo index (for dispatch) and a last-known cache (for tracking). When food is ~ready, dispatch scores nearby fresh/available couriers by ETA/load and offers the order as a time-boxed lease (CAS accept, timeout requeue); the eater live-tracks the assigned courier with a recomputed ETA via push, with location scoped to the active order. Orders shard by id and the geo index/stream by region with per-region dispatch; geo/last-known state is rebuildable from the stream while the order store stays authoritative. Under provider failure the system degrades to coarse ETAs rather than failing matches.

view.caption: Clients work through the gateway and order service, which persists each transition to the order store with a transactional outbox; the order-events stream drives dispatch and notifications. Courier pings feed a region-partitioned location stream that updates the geo index (for dispatch) and a last-known cache (for tracking); the eater live-tracks the assigned courier with ETAs.

functional.how: Location stream last-known + ETA service + push.

nonFunctional.how: Durable state machine + transition log + saga compensation + idempotency keys + CAS on expected state.
Geo index for matching + location stream materializing a last-known cache for tracking.
State machine as saga ledger; idempotent transitions; transactional outbox so events aren't lost or duplicated.
Region/cell sharding + per-region dispatch + partitioned stream + hot-cell splitting + ping backpressure.
Tracking scoped to assigned active orders; bounded retention; no full GPS history in the live index; precision reduced after delivery.
Coarse/heuristic ETA fallback and ping backpressure keep orders flowing when the routing provider fails.

say: Confirm three-sided: eater, restaurant, courier, coordinated by an order lifecycle.
Pin order consistency and real-time matching/tracking as the goals.
Note meal-time spikes and geo concentration.
Order state machine as the source of truth.
Geo index of live courier locations.
Live tracking + ETA from the location stream.
Tradeoffs: dispatch timing vs courier idle, location write volume, saga complexity.
Mention batching and surge pricing as extensions.

expectations: Models an order lifecycle and assigns couriers.
May miss geo indexing, saga compensation, and dispatch timing.
Designs the state machine + geo index + offer/accept dispatch.
Reasons about ready-time-aware dispatch and batching.
Discusses regional sharding, hotspots, and degradation precisely.

followUps: How would you batch multiple orders to one courier efficiently?
How do you set ETAs that blend prep time, travel, and traffic?
How would you add surge pricing to balance supply and demand?
How do you handle a courier who accepts then becomes unreachable?
How would you make dispatch fair across couriers over time?
How do you keep courier location private — who can see it, for how long, and at what precision?
Which metrics would you alert on to catch a dispatch or tracking regression during a meal-time spike?
How do you handle an ambiguous payment-provider callback (auth maybe succeeded) without double-charging?
